Given a reasonable range of appeal a journal that could commang
the loyal services of such minds and pens ought to have gone on jt,
way merrily towards an assured success. It is a thousand pities that
permanency was not its lot. But, much to our intellectual losg and
misfortune, the social and economic conditions of Ireland have alwayg
told against the chances of any Irish magazine or journal or periodical
where hiterature, even with a Nationalist motive force behind 1t, s beey
overshadowed by the rough and combative vigour of political statement

“and invective. ‘‘Zozimus’’ had this handicap to carry and it also

clashed with another antagonism. It sought to make its way on the

‘merits of its own htemry daintiness at a time when men’s minds were

absorbed by, and focussed upon, the lurid spectacle of the life. and
QGat'h-struggle that was involved in the Franco-German War of 1870.
However, notwithstanding these adverse circumstances, the journal
made a bold bid for a healthy life. Under A. M. Sullivan’s manage

“ment it ran, -without a moment’s deterioration in its artistic or

htera'ry quahtv,_,from the 1Sth May, 1870, to the 28th Ootober, 1871,

paper throu ghout.

in the Ireland of that day the triumph of success could no mozz be

Nationalist in i1ts character. The new series had a life of 44 members
running, a8 it did, from the 4th November, 1871, to the 31st August,
1872. ¢‘ Zozimus,”’ therefore, in the entirety of its existence covered
120 issues. | '

The paper as 1t was produced represented a two years’ activity,

Iiterary and artistic, that was emphatically creditable to Irish journal-

ism. The contributions which filled its columns deserve to be regarded
‘aa something more than mere passing or ephemeral jeux d’ esprit.
~ Reading them to-day there is much to' interest and amuse in these
forty-year old pages. From the work of three men, as it was shaped: in
“ Zozimus,”’ selections are now to be found within permanent book-

covers, those three men being T. D. Sullivan, Richard Dowling, _and

Edwin Hamilton. It is a stroke of literary ill-luck that Nugent Robin-
son’s laughable stories and sketches have not, been republished in book

form. When: the journal finally ceasefl publication: Richard Dowling
drifted soom to London to seek his fortune there. John Fergus O'Hea{ R
